hero.headline: Since 1989.
hero.sub: We build software that works. Sometimes it even surprises.

events.text: Zdzisław Krawczyk returns from Libya. Thirty months on a construction contract in Tripoli. That's how you earned serious money back then. Enough dollars to try something of his own.
Poland is born. So are we.
First computer game! It sold… badly.
We switch to office software! Boring, but it pays the bills.
Y2K — the infamous millennium bug. Both us and the payroll system survive. The bugs in the wall panelling, unfortunately, too…
The printer incident. Details sealed by mutual agreement.
We buy a ping-pong table. Nobody plays. Should have bought a foosball table…
Marta joins. As cover. She doesn't know it yet, but she'll stay.
Pandemic. We already worked remotely, so essentially nothing happened.
Hania joins as a junior. On day one, she asks for production access.
Hania builds Skill Issue on a Friday afternoon.
Bożenka takes note. "Hey, this is actually good."
Julka says: we’re growing Skill Issue. And going live.

bozena.name: Bożenka from HR
bozena.title: Head of HR & Administration
bozena.bio: Has outlasted four CEOs, two office moves, and the printer incident. Does not accept CVs with the word "passionate". Does not use the word "passionate".
bozena.quote: CEOs come and go. I stay.

marta.name: Marta
marta.title: Head of Product
marta.bio: Head of Product before anyone here knew what that meant. Makes roadmaps, manages clients, runs Secret Santa with a spreadsheet that has conditional formatting.
marta.quote: I was supposed to stay two years. Ten have passed. I think I like it here.

julka.name: Julka from HR
julka.title: HR Specialist
julka.bio: Joined straight from university. Has already reorganised three processes. Runs a Notion board nobody uses. Has a doc called "suggestions (please read)" — Marta has opened it twice.

zdzislaw.name: Zdzisław Krawczyk
zdzislaw.title: Founder
zdzislaw.bio: Earned it in Libya, came back with capital and a plan. Founded Codexpol in 1989 — computers, then proprietary office software. Handed the company to Mariusz in 2018. His son took over, keeps growing it, travels the world. A loving wife, a house in Konstancin, company in good hands. Says he made it. Hard to disagree.
zdzislaw.quote: I took risks. It worked out. No regrets.

mariusz.name: Mariusz Krawczyk
mariusz.title: CEO
mariusz.bio: Son of Zdzisław. Took over in 2018. MBA from Koźmiński. In 2019 proposed rebranding to "Codeex". Bożenka didn't update the letterhead. The matter was dropped. Has worked hybrid since 2021 — from which country varies. Recently from Bali. Shows up at the Christmas party, in the email footer photo, and when something needs signing that Bożenka can't sign herself. Which happens less and less.

hania.name: Hania Sikorska
hania.title: Mid-level Developer
hania.bio: Studied bioinformatics. Joined as a junior, quiet in meetings, active in pull requests. On a Friday afternoon in 2024 she built a scraper matching job listings to CVs. Told nobody. Bożenka found it on Monday.
hania.quote: Hania is still water. She carves the banks.

tomek.name: Tomek
tomek.title: Tech Lead
tomek.bio: Started as a senior developer, became tech lead because nobody else wanted to run the 9:15 standups. Knows every line of legacy code. Understands some of them. Wears t-shirts with jokes only programmers find funny — and is proud of it.

filip.name: Filip
filip.title: Frontend Developer
filip.bio: The only person in the office with opinions about typography, spacing, and why that shade of blue is wrong. Also does UX, because someone has to. Tomek says it's not his job. Filip disagrees.
filip.quote: Pixel perfect isn't an obsession. It's a standard.

damian.name: Damian
damian.title: Data Analyst
damian.bio: Turns data into charts, charts into insights, insights into presentations. Knows exactly what the numbers say.
damian.quote: People? Sometimes they lie. But numbers and data? Never!

tatiana.name: Tatiana
tatiana.title: Tester
tatiana.bio: Finds bugs in things that "definitely work". Her test list has more items than Filip's wishlist for the next sprint. Even Sylwia and Tomek hold their breath before opening a message from her.
tatiana.quote: Bugs don't sleep. Neither do I.

sylwia.name: Sylwia
sylwia.title: Senior Fullstack Engineer
sylwia.bio: Writes the backend before others finish their coffee, fixes the frontend before anyone opens a ticket. Treats code review as a contact sport. Her comments are short, precise, and mildly terrifying.
sylwia.quote: Refactoring doesn't hurt. Technical debt does.

office.label: Office software
office.text: We've been doing this for 37 years. It's stable, it works, our clients are satisfied. Which clients? They'd prefer not to be named.

skillIssue.label: Skill Issue
skillIssue.text: Hania built it on a Friday. Bożenka said we're taking it. Marta made a roadmap. Julka has feedback.

origin.title: How it started
origin.p1: Hania studied bioinformatics and joined Codexpol in 2022. Quiet in meetings. Opens PRs that make people ask: "when did she even do that?"
origin.p2: On a Friday afternoon in 2024, tired of watching her friends send CVs into the void and get nothing back, she built something. It was a Friday, it was an idea — she told nobody.
origin.p3: Bożenka found it on Monday, in the internal tools folder, looking for the leave request system.
origin.quote: Hania is still water. She carves the banks.
origin.attribution: — Bożenka from HR, after Hania showed her Skill Issue

footer.copy: Codexpol Sp. z o.o. © 1989–2026. All rights reserved. Even the ones from the 90s.

homeAbout.p1: Office software since 1989. Poland changed governments, regulations, and centuries — we just kept coding.
homeAbout.p2: Today we also do network security, AI tooling, and a few things clients would rather we didn't mention.

teamStrip.label: People
teamStrip.kadrySub: Bożenka, Marta and Julka. They keep the order, the processes, and make sure nobody forgets to book their leave.
teamStrip.techSub: Tomek, Sylwia, Hania, Filip, Damian and Tatiana. They write code, analyse data and find bugs — especially Tatiana.
teamStrip.zarzadSub: Zdzisław founded it. Mariusz continues. Recently from Bali.

soon.label: Coming soon
soon.title: Skill Issue
soon.tagline: Your CV as a recruiter, ATS, and the job market actually see it.
features: ATS score with specific improvement tips
Matched job offers from Polish and international boards

products.title: Products
products.subtitle: Office software since 1989. Stable, maintained, used.
products.secret: We make other things too. Some clients prefer we don't mention them.

sigmaFk.name: SIGMA FK
sigmaFk.tagline: Stable as a VAT refund that never arrives. But faster.
sigmaFk.desc: Codexpol's legendary financial and accounting system, which has survived more VAT changes than the average accountant cares to remember. Stable, comprehensive, and launched every morning at 7:59 by accounting departments across Poland.

hermes.name: HERMES Handel Magazyn
hermes.tagline: Survived three warehouse renovations and one brother-in-law from IT.
hermes.desc: A sales and warehouse system that has outlasted three warehouse renovations, five computer replacements, and two attempts at implementing something "modern". Still runs fast even on a machine with an Intel Inside sticker from 2006.

teczka95.name: TECZKA 95
teczka95.tagline: For companies with binders older than some of their employees.
teczka95.desc: Document and personnel file archiving, digital and paper. Running since 1995. The name has not changed since the beginning. Clients know what it is — nothing to discuss.

komBit.name: KOM-BIT Kadry i Płace
komBit.tagline: Everything HR is afraid to touch manually in Excel.
komBit.desc: HR, payroll, ZUS contributions, and leave management in one place. Particularly popular with accounting offices and people who have been clicking here for fifteen years and need it to work.

vatowiec.name: VATOWIEC
vatowiec.tagline: Appears whenever the Finance Ministry changes something "just slightly" again.
vatowiec.desc: VAT and JPK declaration software. A small module with enormous psychological support power for accounting departments. Updated after every regulatory change. There are many.

zapora.name: ZAPORA
zapora.tagline: Stands between your infrastructure and several thousand servers with bad intentions.
zapora.desc: Poland's first dedicated anti-DDoS system. Handles volumetric attacks, SYN floods, and other things whose names sound worse than they are. Bożenka thinks it's "some security program". She's not wrong.

legacy.name: LEGACY
legacy.tagline: Tomek understood it. Tomek is on leave. Now the AI understands it.
legacy.desc: An AI-powered tool for analysing, documenting, and migrating legacy code to modern technologies. Works with COBOL, VB6, Delphi, 'some Perl from 2003', and several other things you're running in production and prefer not to discuss. Legacy doesn't ask why the code looks the way it does. It just reads it.

games.title: Gamedev. We tried.
games.subtitle: Two games. One year. Thirty-four copies.
games.epilogue: After these results we decided to focus on office software. It was the right call.

podroz.name: BUSINESS TRIP
podroz.tagline: Zenon Wróblewski. Logistics clerk. Business trip to Katowice. The invoice is wrong.
podroz.desc: You play a delegate who must sign an incorrect invoice in Katowice. The game starts on the platform at 5:42. It ends when you file your expense report. There is no way to speed this up.
podroz.review: Technically correct. Why it was made — unclear.

wczasy.name: COMPANY HOLIDAY
wczasy.tagline: A week at FWP Ustroń resort. Pool closed since 1988. Breakfast 7:00–7:45.
wczasy.desc: Seven days at a FWP workers' holiday resort. The neighbour in room 304 plays cards until 11pm. Buffet closed from 2pm. Optional trip to the castle — the coach was late. Four endings. None of them good.
wczasy.review: We don't understand why they released a second one.

about.title: About
about.sub: Thirty-seven years. Governments changed, regulations changed, storage formats changed. We kept coding.

kadryPage.title: HR & Admin
kadryPage.sub: The people who keep everything running. Not just the software.

techPage.title: Tech Team
techPage.sub: The people who write it all. And test it. And don't sleep.

zarzadPage.title: Management
zarzadPage.sub: One built it. The other runs it. Bożenka watches both.

items.quote: Twenty years without a migration. Three regulatory changes, two audits, one pandemic. SigmaFK survived it all. I recommend it — and I say that as someone who survived an SAP implementation.
items.name: Krzysztof W.

items.quote: The previous system lost data on every Windows update. Teczka95 has everything from 2001 — leave records, sick notes, amendments. I don't know how. But it does.
items.name: Anna P.

items.quote: Previous implementation: eight months, external consultant, three delays. Hermes: two weeks, our own team, zero delays. The difference speaks for itself.
items.name: Marek D.

items.quote: Twenty years without a migration. Three regulatory changes, two audits, one pandemic. SigmaFK survived it all. I recommend it — and I say that as someone who survived an SAP implementation.
items.name: Krzysztof W.

items.quote: The previous system lost data on every Windows update. Teczka95 has everything from 2001 — leave records, sick notes, amendments. I don't know how. But it does.
items.name: Anna P.

items.quote: Previous implementation: eight months, external consultant, three delays. Hermes: two weeks, our own team, zero delays. The difference speaks for itself.
items.name: Marek D.

items.quote: We had an attack on a Friday evening. Classic timing. Zapora raised the alarm, cut the traffic, sent a report before I managed to order my second course. That's exactly what infrastructure protection should look like.
items.name: Tomasz R.

items.quote: I used to explain to the board what a SYN flood is. Now I show them a chart: red bars are attacks, the green line is uptime. The green line is flat. The board is happy. So am I.
items.name: Marek S.

items.quote: We've never had a serious attack. But I have logs. Last year Zapora blocked 1.2 million suspicious connections. I'd rather not know what it would have looked like without it.
items.name: Krzysztof M.

items.quote: Zbyszek knew how it worked. Zbyszek retired. Legacy now knows more than Zbyszek did. We don't have to call Zbyszek.
items.name: Kamil R.

items.quote: Since 1994 this system has controlled our production line. For thirty years no one knew exactly how — and we asked. Legacy read the code over a weekend and wrote documentation that never existed. On Monday, for the first time, we weren't afraid to touch the code.
items.name: Sławomir P.

items.quote: Before Legacy: a year of work, four developers, a backlog that grew faster than it shrank. After Legacy: three weeks, documentation, a dependency map, a migration plan. Now we develop instead of fighting fires. The team came back to life. People stopped staring at the floor during retrospectives.
items.name: Michał R.

items.quote: I used to do payroll in Excel. Now I have my Saturdays back.
items.name: Marta J.

items.quote: Another VAT change mid-quarter. VATOWIEC knew about it before I did. I'm not sure whether that worries me or reassures me.
items.name: Andrzej P.
